comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a simple, unified iterative framework (PLACECENTER/PLACE/RECENTER) for a broad family of multidimensional scaling (MDS) problems, including classic least-squares MDS, an `L1`-robust variant, and spherical MDS (with either chordal or geodesic distances). The framework reduces each global iteration to independently re-placing individual points via a local min-sum problem, and uses well-known recentering routines (centroid for L2, Weiszfeld-like for L1, Karcher methods on manifolds for spherical L2) to instantiate RECENTER. The paper gives a general convergence argument (descent) for the block-relaxation scheme, reports experiments on synthetic data comparing against SMACOF and the Cayton–Dasgupta REE approach, and proves a Johnson–Lindenstrauss-type dimensionality reduction result for geodesic distances on spheres (project to O((1/epsilon^2) log n) dims while preserving geodesic distances up to (1+epsilon)). Code and data-generation details are provided.

Strengths:
- The algorithmic idea is simple, modular and broadly applicable: many MDS variants become instances by changing the local RECENTER routine. This unification is useful in practice and easy to implement (authors claim very compact MATLAB code).
- The framework is parameter-free (no Lagrange multipliers), which is attractive compared to hand-tuned majorization or SDP-based methods.
- The paper addresses spherical MDS (including geodesic distances), which is less well-covered in prior literature, and adapts known manifold algorithms (Karcher means, Weiszfeld variants) into this local-placement framework.
- The spherical Johnson–Lindenstrauss-type theorem is an interesting theoretical contribution, complementing the algorithmic work.
- Experimental comparisons include reasonable baselines (SMACOF, SMACOF-Q, Cayton–Dasgupta REE+SVD) and show the proposed method consistently finds low-cost embeddings and is competitive runtime-wise on tested instances.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Experiments are limited to synthetic datasets with n=300 and d up to 200; no real-world datasets or larger n are reported. This limits assessment of empirical scalability and practical utility on commonly encountered data sizes.
- The experimental setup favors methods that require an SVD seed; while the authors consider CD with random initialization too, broader baselines (more recent manifold embedding or robust embedding methods) are not included. Sensitivity to initialization and to the stopping threshold t is not thoroughly explored.
- Convergence guarantees are descent-based (monotonic decrease of cost); there is no global optimality guarantee (expected for nonconvex MDS) nor specific rates of convergence. Practical iteration counts and dependence on n,k are discussed but not analyzed rigorously.
- The spherical JL proof is plausible and interesting, but the provided sketch relies on technical lemmas with bounds (e.g., restricting small-angle range x<=0.7) and some constants; a fuller discussion of constants, failure modes, or comparison to manifold-JL literature would strengthen the theoretical claims.
- Presentation has duplicated text fragments in the provided manuscript (likely an artifact of the provided copy) and occasional typos; some algorithmic steps (practical choices in RECENTER on spheres, numerical stability of Weiszfeld-like schemes near data points) would benefit from clearer description.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Include experiments on real datasets (e.g., standard embedding / visualization benchmarks) and larger n (thousands) to show empirical scalability and robustness.
- Provide more details and empirical study of sensitivity to initialization, thresholds, and runtime vs. iteration counts. Report memory usage as n grows.
- Expand comparison with additional baselines where applicable, and consider including ablation studies that isolate the contribution of the local-placement scheme vs. seeding strategy.
- In the theory section, give more commentary on constants and parameter ranges in Theorem 6.1, and compare/contrast more explicitly with existing manifold JL results (e.g., linearization dimension bounds) to clarify the novelty and tightness of the spherical JL bound.
- Clean up presentation (remove duplicated paragraphs, tighten proofs or move technical lemmas to an appendix) and add notes on numerical implementation details (regularization in Weiszfeld updates, convergence tolerances).

Overall assessment:
The paper presents a useful, well-motivated, and practical unification of many MDS variants under a single local-placement framework, with straightforward implementations for Euclidean and spherical target spaces, and a nontrivial theoretical complement in the spherical JL result. The experimental evidence on synthetic data is promising. The contribution is suitable for the community and merits dissemination after addressing the empirical evaluation breadth and improving exposition of some technical parts. I recommend acceptance / publication (highly positive recommendation).
